name: services
context: Author, install, verify, and activate systemd service units for daemons and one-shot jobs
info: This skill covers `.service` unit authoring for both the system manager and per-user managers. It focuses on the fields agents actually need to set to make processes production-safe: `Type=`, `ExecStart=`, restart policy, user and group, working directory, environment, and boot or login activation through `[Install]`. It also covers safe installation flow: write the unit into the correct load path, verify it with `systemd-analyze verify`, run `systemctl daemon-reload`, then enable or start it. Agents use this skill to turn servers, workers, sync processes, and shell automation into managed services with explicit lifecycle and logging behavior.
triggers: create a systemd service for my app
run a script as a linux service
make this process start on boot
convert a command into a managed service
body:
# Service Units

A `.service` file tells systemd how to launch and supervise a process. The common shape is `[Unit]`, `[Service]`, and `[Install]`. For most long-running daemons, `Type=exec` is the safest default because `systemctl start` will fail if the executable or configured user cannot be resolved.

```ini
[Unit]
Description=My App API
After=network-online.target
Wants=network-online.target

[Service]
Type=exec
User=deploy
WorkingDirectory=/srv/my-app
Environment=NODE_ENV=production
ExecStart=/usr/bin/node /srv/my-app/server.js
Restart=on-failure
RestartSec=5s

[Install]
WantedBy=multi-user.target
```

Install system units under `/etc/systemd/system` and user units under `~/.config/systemd/user`. Verify before reload:

```bash
systemd-analyze verify /etc/systemd/system/my-app.service
systemctl daemon-reload
systemctl enable --now my-app.service
```

Use drop-ins instead of editing vendor files in place. A file such as `/etc/systemd/system/my-app.service.d/10-env.conf` can override `Environment=` or `ExecStart=`. See [[systemd/unit-files#overrides-and-dropins]] and [[systemd/troubleshooting#overrides-and-stale-config]].

sections.title: Service unit anatomy
sections.body: Place generic dependency metadata in `[Unit]`, the process contract in `[Service]`, and install targets in `[Install]`. The minimum useful service usually sets `Description=`, `Type=`, `ExecStart=`, and `WantedBy=`. Add `User=`, `Group=`, `WorkingDirectory=`, `Environment=` or `EnvironmentFile=`, and `Restart=` when the process is long-running.

sections.title: Service types
sections.body: `Type=exec` is the recommended default for long-running services because it surfaces executable and user setup failures. Use `Type=oneshot` for jobs that run and exit. Use `Type=notify` or `Type=dbus` only when the service actually implements the required readiness signaling protocol. Avoid legacy `Type=forking` unless the daemon still follows a parent-forks-child model and cannot be modernized.

sections.title: Install flow
sections.body: The reliable flow is: write the unit file into the correct path, run `systemd-analyze verify`, run `systemctl daemon-reload`, then `enable` and/or `start`. Follow with `systemctl show -p FragmentPath -p ActiveState -p SubState --value` and `journalctl -u UNIT --no-pager` to confirm both the loaded path and runtime behavior.

sections.title: Overrides and drop-ins
sections.body: If the distribution or a package owns the primary unit file under `/usr/lib/systemd/system`, do not edit it in place. Create a drop-in under `/etc/systemd/system/NAME.service.d/*.conf` or the equivalent user path. Drop-ins are merged after the main file and are the correct place for environment changes, dependency additions, or `ExecStart=` replacement logic.

scripts.name: Install Service
scripts.description: Render a `.service` unit, verify it, install it into the correct scope, reload the manager, and optionally enable or start it

SYSTEMD_USE_SUDO.description: Use sudo for system-level writes and manager calls

SYSTEMD_SERVICE_NAME.description: Service unit name, with or without the .service suffix

SYSTEMD_SERVICE_TYPE.description: Service type such as exec, oneshot, notify, or dbus

SYSTEMD_SERVICE_USER.description: User to run the service as

SYSTEMD_SERVICE_GROUP.description: Group to run the service as

name.description: Service name, with or without .service suffix

depends.reason: Reloading, enabling, and starting the new unit happens through systemctl
Correct load paths and override rules matter before writing the unit
Verifying the unit before activation prevents a broken deploy
